name: cli
context: Run DuckDB from the command line for SQL queries, file inspection, exports, CI checks, and shell pipelines
info: The DuckDB CLI is a single executable that opens in-memory or persistent `.duckdb` databases, runs SQL with command flags, emits table/CSV/JSON output, and reads data files directly. Agents use this skill when they need deterministic local analytics from shell scripts: query a Parquet or CSV file, create a temporary database, export query results, validate dataset shape in CI, or produce JSON for downstream tools. This skill includes real wrappers for executing SQL and inspecting local or remote files, and it routes deeper learning to [[duckdb/sql]], [[duckdb/files]], [[duckdb/extensions]], [[duckdb/secrets]], and [[duckdb/performance]].
triggers: query this parquet file from shell
body:
# DuckDB CLI

The CLI gives agents a small, deterministic execution surface for local analytics. It can run without a database file, or it can open a persistent `.duckdb` file that stores tables, views, macros, secrets, and extension metadata.

## Installation Check

```bash
duckdb --version
# If missing, install from https://duckdb.org/docs/stable/installation/ or use your package manager.
```

## Invocation Patterns

```bash
# In-memory query
duckdb -c "SELECT 42 AS answer;"

# Persistent database file
duckdb analysis.duckdb -c "CREATE TABLE events AS SELECT * FROM 'events.parquet';"

# Read-only inspection
duckdb -readonly analysis.duckdb -c "SELECT count() FROM events;"

# JSON for downstream agents
duckdb -json -c "SELECT count(*) AS rows FROM 'events.csv';"

# CSV export to stdout
duckdb -csv -c "SELECT * FROM 'events.parquet' LIMIT 10;"
```

The CLI accepts output flags such as `-csv`, `-json`, and `-readonly`. Interactive use also supports dot commands like `.help`, `.tables`, `.schema`, `.mode`, `.output`, `.read`, and `.timer` because the shell is modeled after the SQLite CLI.

## Repository Scripts

```bash
# Execute SQL directly
nodes/duckdb/cli/scripts/run-sql.sh --sql "SELECT count(*) AS rows FROM 'data.csv'" --output json

# Execute a file of SQL against a persistent database
nodes/duckdb/cli/scripts/run-sql.sh --db analysis.duckdb --file report.sql --output table

# Inspect a local or remote data file
nodes/duckdb/cli/scripts/inspect-file.sh data/events.parquet --sample 20 --output table
```

## Operational Rules

- Use `--readonly` when inspecting an existing `.duckdb` file you do not intend to modify.
- Use `-json` when another tool or agent will parse the result.
- Use a SQL file for multi-statement workflows and committed reports.
- Add `ORDER BY` in reports; unordered analytical results are not stable by contract.
- Put credentials in [[duckdb/secrets]], environment variables, or external secret stores, not in committed SQL files.

## Common Shell Recipes

```bash
# Count rows in every Parquet partition as one table
duckdb -json -c "SELECT count(*) AS rows FROM read_parquet('warehouse/events/**/*.parquet');"

# Convert CSV to Parquet
duckdb -c "COPY (SELECT * FROM read_csv('input.csv', union_by_name = true)) TO 'output.parquet' (FORMAT parquet);"

# Fail CI if duplicate IDs exist
duckdb -c "SELECT id, count(*) c FROM 'users.csv' GROUP BY id HAVING c > 1 LIMIT 1;" | tee duplicates.txt
test ! -s duplicates.txt

# Build a view over files for repeated work
duckdb analysis.duckdb <<'SQL'
CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW events AS
SELECT * FROM read_parquet('data/events/**/*.parquet', hive_partitioning = true);
SELECT date_trunc('day', ts) AS day, count() FROM events GROUP BY ALL ORDER BY ALL;
SQL
```

Continue to [[duckdb/sql]] for query syntax, [[duckdb/files]] for file readers and COPY, and [[duckdb/performance]] when queries spill, run out of memory, or scan too slowly.

sections.title: Run SQL from shell
sections.body: Use `duckdb -c "SQL"` for one statement or pipe a SQL file into `duckdb database.duckdb`. The `scripts/run-sql.sh` wrapper normalizes database path, output mode, read-only mode, and direct SQL versus SQL files. Prefer SQL files for multi-step automation because they are reviewable and easier to reproduce.

sections.title: Output modes
sections.body: Use table output for humans, `-json` for agents and API handoffs, and `-csv` for pipelines that load into spreadsheets or other data tools. For large exports, prefer `COPY (...) TO 'file.parquet'` or `COPY (...) TO 'file.csv'` from [[duckdb/files]] instead of printing everything to stdout.

sections.title: In-memory versus persistent databases
sections.body: Without a filename the CLI opens a transient in-memory database. With a filename, DuckDB opens or creates a persistent `.duckdb` file. Use persistent files for reusable tables, views, macros, and checkpoints. Use `-readonly` for inspection to avoid accidental writes.

sections.title: Inspect files before acting
sections.body: The `scripts/inspect-file.sh` wrapper builds a small report for CSV, Parquet, or JSON: row count, schema, summary statistics, and a sample. Run it before writing transformation SQL so type inference, headers, dates, and nested values are visible.

sections.title: Safe automation
sections.body: Quote file paths as SQL string literals, avoid interpolating untrusted SQL, and whitelist dynamic identifiers. For credentials, use [[duckdb/secrets]] or environment variables and keep secret creation SQL out of logs. For large files, set [[duckdb/performance#memory-and-temp]] options before the query.

Python is better when the workflow needs DataFrames or parameters

Shell quoting and exit status handling matter for CLI automation

scripts.name: Run DuckDB SQL
scripts.description: Execute DuckDB SQL from an argument or file with configurable database, output mode, and read-only mode

DUCKDB_DATABASE.description: Default database path if --db is omitted

scripts.name: Inspect Data File
scripts.description: Summarize a CSV, Parquet, or JSON file with DuckDB: row count, schema, statistics, and sample rows

database.description: Optional `.duckdb` database path; omit for in-memory execution

sql.description: SQL statement or statements to execute

sqlFile.description: Path to a SQL file to execute instead of `sql`

stdout.description: Command output in the selected mode

depends.reason: The CLI executes DuckDB SQL and file readers
Scripts are Bash wrappers around the DuckDB executable
Useful for post-processing JSON output from DuckDB
